title: Validation Pipeline
tagline: Auto-spawned checks on every model edit
summary: After any model edit, dbt Wizard automatically spawns a structured validation subagent without being asked. That subagent runs a structured checklist: a read-only SQL sanity check against the warehouse, a deferred dbt run (dbt run --defer --state), schema tests, and a production-vs-development state diff. It reports pass/fail with concrete row-count deltas and flags breaking changes before the change ever reaches a pull request.
whyDifferent: A general AI coding agent edits a file and stops; verification is on you, and "it compiles" is the most it can claim because it only reads text and greps. dbt Wizard's validation pipeline is automatic and operates on live state: it actually builds the changed model with deferral against production, queries the warehouse to compare row counts and grain, and runs the model's tests. The result is a grounded pass/fail with deltas and a breaking-vs-non-breaking verdict, not an optimistic guess from static text.

capabilities.title: Auto-spawn on edit, not on request
capabilities.detail: The moment a model's SQL or schema.yml changes, the validation subagent is spawned by the workflow itself. The SE never types "now validate" — the guardrail fires whether or not they remember to ask.

capabilities.title: Deferred build against production state
capabilities.detail: Runs dbt run --defer --state so only the changed model (and what it needs) builds, with unbuilt upstreams resolved from the production manifest. Fast and cheap dev validation without a full project run.

capabilities.title: Live SQL sanity check
capabilities.detail: Executes read-only SQL directly against the warehouse to confirm the edited model returns rows, holds its declared grain, and has no unexpected NULLs in key columns before trusting the build.

capabilities.title: Prod-vs-dev row-count diff
capabilities.detail: Compares the freshly built dev relation against the production relation and surfaces concrete deltas: total rows added/removed, columns added/dropped, and grain shifts that signal a fan-out or a dropped join.

capabilities.title: Schema test execution
capabilities.detail: Runs the model's unique/not_null/relationship and custom tests as part of the gate, returning per-test pass/fail counts rather than asserting tests would pass.

capabilities.title: Breaking-change verdict before the PR
capabilities.detail: Synthesizes results into a clear breaking vs non-breaking call with the impacted downstream consumers named, handed off via submit_summary so the SE sees the risk before opening the pull request.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__dbt_run
tools.purpose: Structured dbt run invocation used by the validation subagent to build the changed model in dev with deferral, resolving unbuilt upstreams from a production/state manifest. Auto-refreshes the local index on success.

params.name: select
params.desc: Node selector, e.g. int_orders_enriched+ to build the model and its downstream.

params.name: defer
params.desc: When true, unselected upstreams resolve from --state instead of being built.

params.name: state
params.desc: Path to the production manifest used for deferral comparison.

params.name: full_refresh
params.desc: Force a full rebuild of incremental models when needed.

tools.returns: Per-node run status (success/error), rows affected, execution time, and an updated index epoch.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__diff
tools.purpose: Compares local/dev state against production for the edited node, the core prod-vs-dev safety check in the gate. Requires a dbt Cloud token or deferred state.

params.name: node
params.desc: The model to compare, e.g. fct_orders.

params.name: against
params.desc: Target to compare to: prod or a named state.

params.name: primary_key
params.desc: Key column(s) used to align rows for added/removed/changed detection.

tools.returns: Row-count delta (added/removed/changed), column-set delta, grain change flag, and a breaking/non-breaking classification.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse
tools.purpose: Live read-only SQL against the warehouse used for the SQL sanity check: confirm the dev relation returns rows, holds grain, and has no NULLs in keys. No dbt run required.

params.name: sql
params.desc: Read-only SELECT to execute (writes are rejected).

params.name: limit
params.desc: Optional row cap for preview safety.

tools.returns: Result rows as structured JSON plus column types and row count.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__dbt_test
tools.purpose: Runs the schema and data tests attached to the edited model as part of the validation gate, returning per-test outcomes rather than assertions.

params.name: select
params.desc: Test or model selector, e.g. int_orders_enriched.

params.name: store_failures
params.desc: Persist failing rows to a table for inspection.

params.name: defer
params.desc: Resolve untested upstreams from state during the test run.

tools.returns: Per-test pass/fail/warn status with failing row counts and the assertion that failed.

examplePrompts.prompt: Add a support_ticket_count column to int_orders_enriched joined from stg_support_tickets.
examplePrompts.intent: Edit a model and rely on the auto-spawned validation gate to confirm grain is preserved and nothing downstream breaks.

examplePrompts.prompt: I just changed the join in fct_orders from inner to left — is that safe to ship?
examplePrompts.intent: Validate an edited model against production before a PR, catching any fan-out or row-count change.

examplePrompts.prompt: Rename brand to brand_name in stg_products and make sure the validation gate signs off.
examplePrompts.intent: Trigger the automatic validation pipeline on an upstream rename and confirm a breaking/non-breaking verdict.

examplePrompts.prompt: Did the validation agent flag any row-count delta between dev and prod for dim_customers after my edit?
examplePrompts.intent: Inspect the prod-vs-dev diff produced by the auto-spawned validation subagent.

examplePrompts.prompt: Build my changed model with deferral against prod and run its tests before I open the PR.
examplePrompts.intent: Manually echo the gate's deferred build and test steps for an edited model.

examplePrompts.prompt: Confirm int_orders_enriched still has one row per order_id after the support-ticket join.
examplePrompts.intent: Run the SQL grain sanity check the validation subagent uses.

interaction.scenario: An SE adds a support_ticket_count column to int_orders_enriched via a left join to stg_support_tickets. They do not ask for validation — the pipeline auto-spawns the validation subagent, which runs the SQL sanity check, a deferred build, tests, and a prod-vs-dev diff, and catches a fan-out that would have doubled order rows.
interaction.userPrompt: Add a support_ticket_count column to int_orders_enriched joined from stg_support_tickets.

events.body: Add a support_ticket_count column to int_orders_enriched joined from stg_support_tickets.
Editing int_orders_enriched: joining stg_support_tickets on order_id and adding count(ticket_id) as support_ticket_count. Writing the SQL now.

events.code:
select
  o.*,
  t.support_ticket_count
from {{ ref('int_orders_enriched') }} o
left join {{ ref('stg_support_tickets') }} t
  on o.order_id = t.order_id

events.body: Model edit detected. Auto-spawning the validation subagent — no manual trigger needed. Running SQL sanity check, then deferred build, tests, and prod-vs-dev diff.

events.toolArgs: {"sql":"select count(*) as rows, count(distinct order_id) as distinct_orders from dev.int_orders_enriched","limit":1}

events.body: Grain check: rows exceed distinct order_id — fan-out detected.

Grain broke: 24,180 rows vs 18,412 distinct order_id. The join is not 1:1 — stg_support_tickets has multiple tickets per order, so the left join fans out. Continuing the gate to quantify against prod.
Build succeeded but row count rose vs prod baseline.
Breaking: row count up 31%, grain changed.
unique test on order_id now fails.

The validation gate failed before any PR. The raw join fanned out int_orders_enriched from 18,412 to 24,180 rows and broke the order_id unique test. Rewriting as a pre-aggregated CTE so support_ticket_count is computed per order before the join, preserving one row per order_id.

events.code:
with tickets as (
  select order_id, count(ticket_id) as support_ticket_count
  from {{ ref('stg_support_tickets') }}
  group by 1
)
select
  o.*,
  coalesce(t.support_ticket_count, 0) as support_ticket_count
from {{ ref('int_orders_enriched') }} o
left join tickets t on o.order_id = t.order_id

events.body: Non-breaking: same row count, one new column.

First attempt was caught automatically — the validation subagent fired the moment I edited the model, not because you asked. The naive join fanned out int_orders_enriched to 24,180 rows and failed the order_id unique test (a breaking change that would have doubled downstream order counts in fct_orders and dim_customers). I rewrote it with a per-order pre-aggregation. Re-validation: grain preserved at 18,412 rows, support_ticket_count added, all 4 tests pass, prod-vs-dev classified non-breaking. Safe to open the PR. Handing off via submit_summary with the impact analysis.

seNotes: Lead with "you didn't ask for it." The demo lands hardest when you visibly skip any validate command and the subagent spawns on its own the instant the model is edited — that auto-guardrail is the differentiator versus a general coding agent.

Show the deferred build: dbt run --defer --state means only the changed model builds while upstreams resolve from the prod manifest, so validation is fast and cheap even on a large project. SEs will ask about cost; this is the answer.

The strongest moment is the prod-vs-dev diff catching a grain/fan-out change with a real row-count delta (e.g. 18,412 to 24,180). That is something a file-reading agent cannot know because it never queries the warehouse.
Make clear the verdict is breaking vs non-breaking with named downstream consumers, delivered before the PR via submit_summary — not after CI fails. The value is shifting failure left.

All four steps are grounded actions: warehouse runs read-only SQL, dbt_run actually builds, dbt_test actually runs the tests, diff actually compares state. Stress to a technical audience that nothing here is the model guessing from text.
